elements.sent-2411-001: This paper presents an accelerated spherical K-means clustering algorithm for large-scale and high-dimensional sparse document data sets.
elements.sent-2411-002: We design an algorithm working in an architecture-friendly manner (AFM), which is a procedure of suppressing performance-degradation factors such as the numbers of instructions, branch mispredictions, and cache misses in CPUs of a modern computer system.
elements.sent-2411-003: For the AFM operation, we leverage unique universal characteristics (UCs) of a data-object and a cluster's mean set, which are skewed distributions on data relationships such as Zipf's law and a feature-value concentration phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-004: The UCs indicate that the most part of the number of multiplications for similarity calculations is executed regarding terms with high document frequencies (df) and the most part of a similarity between an object- and a mean-feature vector is obtained by the multiplications regarding a few high mean-feature values.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our proposed algorithm applies an inverted-index data structure to a mean set, extracts the specific region with high-df terms and high mean-feature values in the mean-inverted index by newly introduced two structural parameters, and exploits the index divided into three parts for efficient pruning.
elements.sent-2411-006: The algorithm determines the two structural parameters by minimizing the approximate number of multiplications related to that of instructions, reduces the branch mispredictions by sharing the index structure including the two parameters with all the objects, and suppressing the cache misses by keeping in the caches the frequently used data in the foregoing specific region, resulting in working in the AFM.
elements.sent-2411-007: We experimentally demonstrate that our algorithm efficiently achieves superior speed performance in large-scale documents compared with algorithms using the state-of-the-art techniques.
elements.sent-2411-008: Large collections of text documents have been leveraged in a variety of fields.
elements.sent-2411-009: Understanding their latent structures helps us effectively utilize such document collections.
elements.sent-2411-010: Clustering is one useful method for revealing these structures.
elements.sent-2411-011: Among clustering algorithms, a $K$-means clustering called Lloyd's algorithm \cite{lloyd,macqueen , which is classic yet simple and practical, has been widely used for over 50 years and has been applied to a part of various algorithms such as spectral clustering \cite{ng,vonLuxburg and neural-network compression \cite{wu,cho .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: It partitions an object data set into $K$ distinct clusters with a given number $K$ by locally minimizing an objective function in an iterative greedy manner.
elements.sent-2411-013: For document-data sets, its specialized variant is known as a spherical $K$-means clustering algorithm \cite{dhillon,dhillon_book .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The spherical $K$-means clustering algorithm consists of two main steps, assignment and update, just like Lloyd's algorithm; it differs in its given object-feature vectors and a similarity definition.
elements.sent-2411-015: Feature vector ${\bm x _i$ $(i\!=\! 1,2,\cdots\!,N)$ is represented as a point on a unit hypersphere, i.e., $\|{\bm x _i\|_2\!=\! 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Mean-feature vector ${\bm \mu _j^{[r]}$ of the $j$th cluster $(j\!=\! 1,2,\cdots\!,K)$, which is calculated at the update step in the $r$th iteration, is also normalized by its $L_2$-norm, resulting in $\|{\bm \mu _j^{[r]}\|_2\!=\! 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: A similarity between an object- and a mean-feature vector is defined by the cosine similarity, which is an inner product in this case.
elements.sent-2411-018: Note that the $j$th cluster's mean can be referred to as the centroid to avoid confusion with a mathematical mean.
elements.sent-2411-019: In the spherical $K$-means setting, we solve a $K$-means clustering problem for large-scale and high-dimensional sparse document-data sets with a huge number of $K$.
elements.sent-2411-020: In a typical data set, we deal with the following: number of documents $N\!>\!1\!\times\!10^6$, number of distinct terms in the data set (dimensionality) $D\!>\!1\!\times\!10^5$, average number of distinct terms per document $\hat{D}\sim 100$, and $K\!\sim\!N/100$.
elements.sent-2411-021: For fine-grained analyses on a large-scale documents \cite{knittel , a huge number of $K$ is required in the normal course of events.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Hereinafter, we respectively call data sets with $(\hat{D /D)\!\ll 1$ and $(\hat{D /D)\!\sim\! 1$ {\em sparse and {\em dense \footnote{ Although the definitions of {\em sparse and {\em dense data are vague, such language has been used in many studies in a similar manner.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: For instance, the indicators $(\hat{D /D)$ in the previous work \cite{huang,schubert are from $2\!\times\!10^{-7}$ to $4.6\!\times\!10^{-3}$ and that in another \cite{li is around $5\!\times\!10^{-2}$.. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Such data sets and their clustering results have interesting {\em universal characteristics (UCs) of skewed forms regarding some quantities such as Zipf's law (Section~\ref{sec:dchar ).}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Among them, a {\em feature-value concentration phenomenon is especially impressive, i.e., a cluster is annotated by one or a few dominant terms (words) with very large feature values.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Owing to them, a relationship between a similarity and its calculation cost becomes like the Pareto principle, i.e., a large fraction of the similarity is calculated at low cost (Section~\ref{sec:dchar ).}
elements.sent-2411-027: Furthermore, a noteworthy phenomenon also appears, {\em initial-state independence , i.e., an initial-state selection (seeding) for clustering does not affect algorithm's performance, especially if a $K$ value is large as in our setting (Section~\ref{sec:dchar and Appendix~\ref{app:init ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Thus, the UCs play an important role in our algorithm design and evaluation as well as can be a scientific research subject from a viewpoint of knowledge discovery.
elements.sent-2411-029: The Lloyd's algorithm has been improved so as to operate at high speed and the smallest possible memory size \cite{kaukoranta,elkan,hamerly,hamerly_book,drake,ding,rysavy,newling .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Such {\em accelerations are, however, for not sparse but dense data sets in a metric space.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Note that the term {\em acceleration means only to speed up an algorithm while keeping the same solution as Lloyd's algorithm if the algorithms start with identical initial states.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Then clustering accuracy is no longer a performance measure.
elements.sent-2411-033: The acceleration in the foregoing algorithms originates in reducing costly exact distance calculations between objects and centroids with pruning methods based on the triangle inequality in a metric space.
elements.sent-2411-034: We briefly explain a typical usage of the triangle inequality and point out its disadvantage.
elements.sent-2411-035: Consider skipping a distance calculation between object ${\bm x _i$ and current target centroid ${\bm \mu _j^{[r]}$ by using a lower bound on the distance.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: We can do it if the lower bound is larger than a distance between ${\bm x _i$ and a centroid of the cluster which ${\bm x _i$ is assigned to.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: By applying the triangle inequality to three points of ${\bm x _i$, ${\bm \mu _j^{[r]}$, and the previous centroid ${\bm \mu _j^{[r-1]}$, the lower bound is calculated as \begin{equation}d_{LB}({\bm x}_i, {\bm \mu}_j^{[r]}) = |d({\bm x}_i,{\bm \mu}_j^{[r-1]})\!-\!\delta_j^{[r]}|\,, \label{eq:lb}\end{equation} where $\delta_j^{[r]}$ denotes the moving distance between ${\bm \mu _j^{[r-1]}$ and ${\bm \mu _j^{[r]}$ and $d({\bm a ,{\bm b )$ and $d_{LB ({\bm a ,{\bm b )$ respectively denote the distances of ${\bm a}$ to ${\bm b}$ and the lower bound (detailed in Appendix~\ref{app:triangle ).}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The lower bound tightens as the moving distance becomes smaller.
elements.sent-2411-039: Then more centroids are pruned, causing acceleration.
elements.sent-2411-040: However, such acceleration algorithms have an essential limit, that is, they become effective only around the last stage before the convergence where most of the centroids are invariant or slightly move.
elements.sent-2411-041: Suppose that we prepare an algorithm by replacing the distance with the cosine similarity and utilizing a similar pruning strategy for the foregoing accelerations \cite{schubert .}
elements.sent-2411-042: In our setting, it is not efficient as stated above (detailed in Section~\ref{sec:arch ).}
elements.sent-2411-043: For further improvement, it is desired that the acceleration goes through all the iterations, particularly from the early to the middle stage.
elements.sent-2411-044: We challenge the efficient acceleration of the spherical $K$-means in our setting, under the condition of it operates in a modern computer system with CPUs.
elements.sent-2411-045: Our idea is to make an algorithm to operate in an {\em architecture-friendly manner (AFM) through all the iterations by leveraging the universal characteristics (UCs) for {\em data structures and {\em pruning filters .}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: An algorithm operating in the AFM uses as few microcode instructions issued in the CPU as possible, preventing pipeline hazards that degrade computational performance, i.e., suppressing performance-degradation factors including the multiplications for similarity calculations\footnote{ The number of multiplications is closely related to the number of microcode instructions regarding similarity calculations and can be directly monitored in real time.. (Section~\ref{sec:arch ).}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Regarding the data structure, we employ an inverted-index data structure as in similarity search algorithms for large-scale documents \cite{zezula , which utilize it for a database \cite{harman,knuth,samet,zobel,buttcher .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Our inverted index is characterized by its usage and novel structure: it is applied not to an object-feature-vector set but to a mean-feature-vector set since it effects better performance \cite{aoyama_jdsa and partitioned into three regions with two structural parameters (separators or thresholds) for an efficient pruning filter based on the UCs.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Furthermore, an object is represented with a tuple (term ID,\,object-feature value) called sparse expression, where the term IDs are given in ascending order of the document frequency.
elements.sent-2411-050: We utilize two pruning filters; a novel main and a conventional auxiliary one.
elements.sent-2411-051: The main filter exploits a unique upper bound on a similarity, which is calculated by utilizing both the {\em summable property of the inner product (similarity) and an {\em estimated shared (ES) threshold on mean-feature values, based on the foregoing data structures (Sections~\ref{sec:prop and \ref{sec:strparam ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Besides our upper-bound-based pruning (UBP) filter, we can consider other UBP filters with the state-of-the-art techniques such as a threshold algorithm (TA) in the similarity-search field \cite{fagin01,li and blockification using the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality (CS) \cite{bottesch (Section~\ref{ssec:perfcomp ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Our UBP filter differs from the TA and CS in ways that its threshold is {\em estimated by minimizing an approximate number of multiplications for similarity calculations and is {\em shared with all the objects.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: For this reason, we call our UBP filter ES.
elements.sent-2411-055: Our auxiliary filter is called an invariant-centroid-based pruning (ICP) filter \cite{kaukoranta,bottesch,hattori .}
elements.sent-2411-056: It becomes effective toward the last stage in the iterations.
elements.sent-2411-057: It is a sort of accelerations that uses the moving distance (Section~\ref{ssec:icp ).}
elements.sent-2411-058: By combining both the filters, our algorithm works in the AFM through all the iterations.
elements.sent-2411-059: In a similar setting to ours, an algorithm has been reported which uses a mean-inverted index and pruning filters consisting of a UBP filter based on the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality and a simpler ICP filter \cite{knittel .}
elements.sent-2411-060: Our proposed algorithm is evaluated compared with similar algorithms with UBP filters based on the TA and CS combined with the auxiliary ICP filter in Section~\ref{ssec:perfcomp .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Note that the following strategies are out of our challenge while they are useful for speeding up: fast heuristics and approximations that don't give the same solution as Lloyd's algorithm \cite{broder14,dongmei_zhang and initial-state selections (seeding) that may lead to fast convergence \cite{arthur,bahmani,min_li,ji but did not affect the performance in our preliminary experiments (Section~\ref{sec:dchar and Appendix~\ref{app:init ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Since the strategies are orthogonal to our algorithm, they don't conflict with ours but rather can be merged into it.
elements.sent-2411-064: We propose an accelerated spherical $K$-means clustering algorithm for large-scale and high-dimensional document-data sets with a huge $K$ value (Section~\ref{sec:prop ).}
elements.sent-2411-065: Our proposed algorithm drastically reduces the elapsed time required at the assignment step in each iteration by suppressing the performance-degradation factors including the number of multiplications.
elements.sent-2411-066: The algorithm is supported by the interrelated data structures and pruning filters based on the universal characteristics (UCs) of the data sets (Section~\ref{sec:dchar ), resulting in its operation in the architecture-friendly manner (AFM) (Section~\ref{sec:arch ).}}
elements.sent-2411-067: We develop an algorithm for determining two structural parameters that partition the mean-inverted index into three regions (Section~\ref{sec:strparam ).}
elements.sent-2411-068: Based on a newly introduced computational model, the algorithm simultaneously finds structural-parameter values at which the approximate number of multiplications is minimum.
elements.sent-2411-069: We experimentally demonstrate that our proposed algorithm achieves superior performance on speed and memory consumption when it is applied to large-scale real document-data sets with large $K$ values and compare it with other similar algorithms (Section~\ref{sec:exp ).}
elements.sent-2411-070: The remainder of this paper consists of the following eight sections.
elements.sent-2411-071: Section \ref{sec:arch describes the design of both the data structure and an algorithm operating in the AFM.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Section \ref{sec:dchar shows the UCs of a large-scale high-dimensional sparse document-data set and its clustering results.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Section \ref{sec:prop explains our proposed algorithm in detail, and Section \ref{sec:strparam describes how to estimate the two structural parameters that are separators of the mean-inverted index.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Section~\ref{sec:exp shows our experimental settings and compares our algorithm's performance with others.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Section \ref{sec:disc discusses our proposed clustering algorithm based on experimental results, and Section \ref{sec:relate reviews related work from viewpoints that clarify the distinct aspects of our work.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The final section provides a conclusion and future work.

elements.sent-2411-078: We consider running an algorithm on a modern computer system with CPUs.
elements.sent-2411-079: An architecture-friendly manner (AFM) is a procedure that suppresses the following three performance-degradation factors: (1) the instructions, (2) the conditional branch mispredictions, and (3) the cache misses.
elements.sent-2411-080: These factors impact the performance of algorithms executed on a modern computer system, which contains CPUs and a hierarchical memory system as its main components.
elements.sent-2411-081: A CPU has plural operating cores each of which has deep pipelines for instruction parallelism with superscalar out-of-order execution and multilevel cache hierarchy \cite{hennepat,intel_IA64 .}
elements.sent-2411-082: The memory system consists of registers, hierarchical caches from level 1 to the last level (e.g., level 3) \cite{intel_IA64 , and an external main memory.}
elements.sent-2411-083: To efficiently run an algorithm at high throughput on the system, we should not only reduce instructions but also avoid pipeline hazards that cause pipeline stalls.
elements.sent-2411-084: In pipeline hazards, there are two serious threats: a control hazard induced by branch mispredictions \cite{evers,eyerman and a data hazard that needs access to the main memory by last-level-cache misses \cite{hennepat .}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Suppressing the three performance-degradation factors creates a high-performance algorithm.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \newcommand{\algto}{to} \algnewcommand{\LineComment}[1]{\Statex \(\hskip.8em\triangleright\) #1} \caption{\hskip.3em Assignment step of MIVI} \label{algo:mivi_assign} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Statex{Input:\hskip.8em $\hat{\mathcal{X}}$,~~$\breve{\mathcal{M}}^{[r-1]}$,~~ $\left\{\rho_{a(i)}^{[r-1]}\right\}_{i=1}^N$,\hskip.3em $\forall j;\hskip.3em C_{j}^{[r]}\leftarrow\emptyset$ } \Statex{Output:\hskip.8em $\mathcal{C}^{[r]}\!=\!\left\{ C_j^{[r]} \right\}_{j=1}^K$} \ForAll{~$\hat{\bm{x}}_i\!=\![(t_{(i,p)},u_{t_{(i,p)}})]_{p=1}^{(nt)_i}$, \hskip.3em$|\hat{\mathcal X}|\!=\!N$\hskip.3em} \label{algo:mivi_outermost_start} \State{$\left\{ \rho_j \right\}_{j=1}^K \gets\! 0$, \hskip.8em $\rho_{(max)}\!\gets\! \rho_{a(i)}^{[r-1]}$} \ForAll{~$[t_{(i,p)}]_{p=1}^{(nt)_i}$\hskip.3em} \ForAll{~$[v_{c_{(s,q)}}]_{q=1}^{(mf)_s}\!\in \breve{\xi}_s^{[r-1]}$,\hskip.2em $s\!=\!t_{(i,p)}$} \State{$\rho_{c_{(s,q)}}\gets \rho_{c_{(s,q)}}+u_s\cdot v_{c_{(s,q)}}$} \label{algo:mivi_outermost_end} \EndFor \EndFor \For{~$j\gets 1$ \algto\hskip.6em $K$\hskip.3em} \State{{\bf If}\hskip.3em$\rho_{j} > \rho_{(max)}$\hskip.3em{\bf then} \hskip.3em$\rho_{(max)}\leftarrow \rho_{j},\hskip.4em a(i)\leftarrow j$} \EndFor \State{$C_{a(i)}^{[r]}\leftarrow C_{a(i)}^{[r]}\cup\{\hat{\bm{x}}_i\}$} \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-086: We describe the impact of branch mispredictions and last-level-cache misses, comparing the following three algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-087: A baseline algorithm utilizes an inverted-index data structure for a mean (centroid) set and adopts the term-at-a-time (TAAT) strategy \cite{fontoura for similarity calculations, which is called a mean-inverted-index algorithm (MIVI) in Algorithm~\ref{algo:mivi_assign \cite{aoyama_jdsa .}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: The second differs from MIVI only in using an inverted index for a given data-object set, which is called a data-inverted-index algorithm (DIVI).
elements.sent-2411-089: The last is an algorithm Ding$^+$ \cite{ding , which is originally for dense data sets and works at high speed using its pruning filters based on the triangle inequality.}
elements.sent-2411-090: To make Ding$^+$ available to a sparse data set in the spherical $K$-means setting, we modified it without any inverted index as follows.
elements.sent-2411-091: A given data-object and a mean set were represented with sparse and full expression, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-092: In the full expression, all the term IDs were covered, where feature values of undefined term IDs were filled by zeros.
elements.sent-2411-093: These representations enable us to simply and quickly access a mean-feature value by using a data-object term ID as a key for a similarity calculation\footnote{ Mean-feature vectors may also be represented with sparse expression.}
elements.sent-2411-094: However, these representations require solving a set-intersection problem of finding terms shared by both a data-object- and a mean-feature vector.. .
elements.sent-2411-095: As is obvious, Ding$^+$ used a cosine similarity as with an algorithm \cite{schubert that is modified from Hamerly's algorithm \cite{hamerly .}}

elements.sent-2411-096: Figure \ref{fig:ivfd and Table \ref{table:ivfd show the performance comparisons of MIVI, DIVI, and Ding$^+$ in the 8.2M-sized PubMed data set (Section \ref{ssec:data ) \cite{pubmed with $K\!=\! 80\,000$ until the convergence.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: The performance rates of DIVI and Ding$^+$ to MIVI are shown in Table \ref{table:ivfd .}
elements.sent-2411-098: The columns from left to right show the algorithm (Algo), the average number of multiplications (Avg Mult), the average elapsed time (Avg time), the numbers of completed instructions (Inst), branch mispredictions (BM), and last-level-cache load misses (LLCM).
elements.sent-2411-099: The entries in the last three columns were measured with Linux perf tools \cite{perf .}
elements.sent-2411-100: Both MIVI and DIVI required an identical number of multiplications for similarity calculations.
elements.sent-2411-101: However, their average elapsed times were critically different.
elements.sent-2411-102: Surprisingly, DIVI needed almost 10 times more average elapsed time than MIVI.
elements.sent-2411-103: Ding$^+$ reduced the multiplications by its pruning method, resulting in almost one-fourth the average number of MIVI's multiplications.
elements.sent-2411-104: However, its average elapsed time was about three times larger than MIVI's.
elements.sent-2411-105: These results reveal that only the number of multiplications or instructions is insufficient for the speed-performance criterion.
elements.sent-2411-106: Table~\ref{table:ivfd also shows that the numbers of branch mispredictions and cache misses of DIVI and Ding$^+$ were significantly larger than those of MIVI.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The branch misprediction and the cache miss cause many wasted clock-cycle times, resulting in slower operation.
elements.sent-2411-108: This can be explained as follows.
elements.sent-2411-109: When a target-data-object does not exist in the caches, a last-level-cache miss occurs and the object is loaded from the main memory in the large memory latency.
elements.sent-2411-110: Such a situation is generally caused by the poor temporal and spatial locality in data usage.
elements.sent-2411-111: DIVI replaces the triple loop of MIVI at lines~\ref{algo:mivi_outermost_start to \ref{algo:mivi_outermost_end in Algorithm~\ref{algo:mivi_assign as follows: the outermost loop is for mean-feature vectors, the middle loop is for the terms in a mean-feature vector whose number is much larger than $(nt)_i$ of MIVI, and the innermost loop is for object-inverted-index arrays whose length is much longer than $(mf)_s$ of $\breve{\xi _s^{[r-1]}$ in MIVI (symbols in Table~\ref{table:nota ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: This replacement loses the locality of target data.

elements.sent-2411-128: The Zipf's law is known as one of universal characteristics \cite{adamic,newman,clauset .}
elements.sent-2411-129: It states that a relationship between an occurrence frequency of a term (or word) in a text corpus (document data set) and the term's rank in descending order of the frequency is empirically represented in a rank range by \begin{equation}\mbox{{\em Freq}(term)}\propto \mbox{{\em Rank}(term)}^{-\alpha}, \label{eq:zipf}\end{equation} where the functions, {\em Freq (term) and {\em Rank (term), are the frequency and rank of the term, and $\alpha$ is a positive exponent.}}
elements.sent-2411-130: In very large data sets, unlike the foregoing simple Zipf's law, it is known that a rank-frequency plot in log-log scale has two parts with different exponents \cite{montemurro .}
elements.sent-2411-131: Figure~\ref{fig:dchar (a) shows Zipf's law of the 8.2M-sized PubMed data set (Section~\ref{ssec:data ), where {\em tf and {\em df respectively denote the total occurrence frequency of each term in all of the documents and the number of documents in which each term appeared.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-132: We know that what follows Zipf's law was not only the term frequency ({\em tf}\,), which is usually said, but also the document frequency ({\em df}\,).
elements.sent-2411-133: Figure~\ref{fig:dchar (b) shows the relationship between the mean frequency ({\em mf ) and {\em Rank (term), where {\em mf is the number of means (centroids) in which a distinct term appeared.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: The log-log plots indicates that although the maximum mean frequencies were bounded by the corresponding $K$ values, the relationship in the mean set also followed Zipf's law, which we call a bounded Zipf's law.
elements.sent-2411-135: The relationships in Figs.~\ref{fig:dchar (a) and (b) are highly right-skewed forms expressed by power functions with negative exponents.}

elements.sent-2411-159: Our proposed algorithm ES-ICP prunes centroids that cannot be the most similar one to reduce the multiplications for similarity calculations (more exactly, the multiply-add operations).

elements.sent-2411-209: Our proposed estimation algorithm minimizes objective function $J$ of the approximate number of the multiplications for similarity calculations $\tilde{\phi}$ (derived in Appendix~\ref{app:objfunct ), which is expressed by \begin{equation}( t^{},v^{} ) = \argmin_{\substack{v_h^{}\in V^{} s_{(min)}\leq s'\leq D}} \left(\, J(s',v_h^{})\,\right) \label{eq:2params}\end{equation} \begin{eqnarray}J(s',v_h^{}) = \tilde{\phi}_{(s',h)} \nonumber = (\phi 1)_{s'} +(\phi 2)_{(s',h)} +(\tilde{\phi}3)_{(s',h)}\, , \label{eq:objfunct2}\end{eqnarray} where $s'$ is a candidate of $t^{}$, $s_{(min)}$ the predetermined minimum value of $s'$, and $v_h^{}$ the $h$th candidate of $v^{}$ that is prepared before ES-ICP starts.}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}( t^{},v^{} ) = \argmin_{\substack{v_h^{}\in V^{} s_{(min)}\leq s'\leq D}} \left(\, J(s',v_h^{})\,\right) \label{eq:2params}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-210: The numbers of multiplications in Regions 1 and 2, $(\phi 1)_{s'}$ and $(\phi 2)_{(s',h)}$, are expressed by \begin{eqnarray} (\phi 1)_{s'} = \sum_{s=1}^{s'-1} (df)_s\cdot (mf)_s \label{eq:cost1} (\phi 2)_{(s',h)} = \sum_{s=s'}^D (df)_s\cdot (mfH)_{(s,v_h^{})}\, , \label{eq:cost2}\end{eqnarray} where $(\phi 1)_{s'}\!+(\phi 2)_{(s',h)}$ is exactly determined, given the tentative $s'$ and $v_h^{}$.
elements.sent-2411-211: By contrast, it is difficult to exactly determine with low computational cost the number of multiplications in Region 3.
elements.sent-2411-212: To approximate this number so as to compute with low cost, we introduce a probability that a centroid passes through the ES filter, which corresponds to $|{\mathcal Z _i|/K$, and express an approximate number as the expected value $(\tilde{\phi 3)_{(s',h)}$\,: \begin{equation}(\tilde{\phi} 3)_{(s',h)} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (ntH)_{(i;s')}\! \cdot K\!\cdot Prob\left(\rho^{[ub]}(i)\!\geq\! \rho_{a(i)}\,;s',\!h \right), \label{eq:cost3}\end{equation} where $(ntH)_{(i;s')}$ is the number of the terms whose IDs are more than or equal to $s'$, $\rho^{[ub] (i)$ denotes the distribution function of the upper-bound similarity of the $i$th object (Appendix~\ref{app:est ), and $\mbox{Prob (\rho^{[ub] (i)\!\geq\! \rho_{a(i)}\,;s',h)$ is the probability of $\rho^{[ub] (i)\!\geq \rho_{a(i)}$, given $s'$ and $h$ (used instead of $v_h^{}$).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-213: The probability is represented by \begin{equation}Prob\,\left( \rho^{[ub]}(i)\!\geq \rho_{a(i)}\,;s',h \right) =\left( \frac{1}{K}\right)\, \left( \frac{K}{e} \right)^{\frac{\Delta\bar{\rho}(i;s',h)}{\rho_{a(i)} -\bar{\rho}_i} } \label{eq:prob_unpruned1}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\Delta\bar{\rho}(i;s',h) = \bar{\rho}^{[ub]}(i\,;s',h) -\bar{\rho}_i \, , \label{eq:Deltabarrho}\end{equation} where $\bar{\rho ^{[ub] (i\,;s',h)$ denotes the average upper bound on the similarities of the $i$th object to all the centroids and $\bar{\rho _i$ denotes the average similarity to them (derived in Appendix~\ref{app:est ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-214: Then Eq.~(\ref{eq:cost3 ) is rewritten as \begin{equation}(\tilde{\phi} 3)_{(s',h)} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (ntH)_{(i,s')} \cdot \left( \frac{K}{e} \right)^{\frac{\Delta\bar{\rho}(i;s',h)}{\rho_{a(i)} -\bar{\rho}_i} }\! . \label{eq:simple_cost3}\end{equation} By substituting Eqs.~(\ref{eq:cost1 ), (\ref{eq:cost2 ), and (\ref{eq:simple_cost3 ) into Eq.~(\ref{eq:objfunct2 ), the objective function is expressed by { \begin{equation}J(s',v_h^{}) = \sum_{s=1}^{s'-1} (df)_s\cdot (mf)_s +\!\sum_{s=s'}^D (df)_s (mfH)_{(s,v_h^{})} \nonumber\end{equation} \begin{equation}+\!\sum_{i=1}^N\, (ntH)_{(i,s')}\cdot \left( \frac{K}{e} \right)^{\frac{ \Delta\bar{\rho}(i;s',h)}{\rho_{a(i)} -\bar{\rho}_i} } \! . \label{eq:objfunct_all}\end{equation} We obtain the two structural parameters of $t^{}$ and $v^{}$ by efficiently solving Eq.~(\ref{eq:2params ) using the objective function in Eq.~(\ref{eq:objfunct_all ) (detailed in Appendix~\ref{app:est ).}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-215: We first describe the data sets and the extracted feature values, followed by a platform for the algorithm evaluation and performance measures.
elements.sent-2411-216: We next compare our ES-ICP with the baseline MIVI and three algorithms, ICP, TA-ICP, and CS-ICP, which were simply extended from existing search and clustering algorithms based on the state-of-the-art techniques in our setting \cite{fagin01,li,bottesch,knittel .}
elements.sent-2411-217: ICP employs only the auxiliary filter designed so as to operate in the AFM.
elements.sent-2411-218: TA-ICP and CS-ICP incorporate their main UBP filters besides the ICP filter.
elements.sent-2411-219: For its main filer, TA-ICP modifies the threshold algorithms (TA) in the search algorithms in Fagin$^+$ \cite{fagin01 and Li$^+$ \cite{li .}}
elements.sent-2411-220: CS-ICP utilizes the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, which is widely used for making upper bounds on an inner product of the vectors \cite{bottesch,knittel .}
elements.sent-2411-221: TA-ICP and CS-ICP are described in Section~\ref{ssec:perfcomp and detailed in Section~\ref{app:compalgo .}}
elements.sent-2411-222: We employed two different types of large-scale and high-dimensional sparse real document data sets: {\em PubMed Abstracts (PubMed) \cite{pubmed and {\em The New York Times Articles (NYT).}}}
elements.sent-2411-223: PubMed contains 8\,200\,000 documents (data objects) each of which was represented by term (distinct word) counts, called 8.2M-sized PubMed.
elements.sent-2411-224: The data set contained terms corresponding to the dimensionality of 141\,043.
elements.sent-2411-225: The average number of non-zero elements in the objects was 58.96, and the sparsity indicator ($\hat{D /D$) (Section~\ref{sec:intro ) was $4.18\!\times\! 10^{-4}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-226: By contrast, the average number of non-zero elements in the centroids was 2094.94, which is 35.53 times larger than that in the objects, when $K\!=\!8\!\times\! 10^4$.
elements.sent-2411-227: Note that mean-feature vectors are still sparse although they contain more terms than object-feature vectors on average.
elements.sent-2411-228: Regarding NYT, we extracted 1\,285\,944 articles (data objects) from {\em The New York Times Articles from 1994 to 2006 and counted the occurrence frequencies of the terms after stemming and stop word removal.}
elements.sent-2411-229: The number of resultant terms in all the objects was 495\,126.
elements.sent-2411-230: The average number of non-zero elements in the objects was 225.76, where $\hat{D /D\!=\!4.56\!\times\!10^{-4}$.}
elements.sent-2411-231: The average number of non-zero elements in the centroids was 5105.73, which is 22.62 times larger than that in the objects when $K\!=\!1\!\times\! 10^4$.
elements.sent-2411-232: We made an object-feature vector from the occurrence frequencies of the terms in the data set.
elements.sent-2411-233: An element of the feature vector was the value of the classic {\em tf-idf (term frequency-inverse document frequency), normalized by the $L_2$-norm of the feature vector.}
elements.sent-2411-234: The {\em tf-idf}\, value of the $s$th term in the $i$th document was defined as \begin{equation}\em tf-idf\,(s,i) = \em tf\,(s,i)\times \log\left( \frac{N}{(df)_s} \right)\: , \label{eq:tf-idf}\end{equation} where {\em tf}\,$(s,i)$ denotes the raw counts of the $s$th term in the $i$th document and $(df)_s$ denotes the document frequency of the $s$th term.
elements.sent-2411-235: Due to normalization, each feature vector was regarded as a point on a unit hypersphere.
elements.sent-2411-236: The algorithms were executed on a computer system that was equipped with two Xeon E5-2697v3 2.6-GHz CPUs with three-level caches from levels 1 to 3 (last level) and 256-GB main memory, by multithreading with OpenMP \cite{openmp of 50 threads within the memory capacity.}
elements.sent-2411-237: The CPU performed the out-of-order superscalar execution with eight issue widths.
elements.sent-2411-238: The algorithms were implemented in C and compiled with a GNU C compiler version 8.2.0 on the optimization level of {\sf -O3 .}
elements.sent-2411-239: The performances were evaluated with the following measurements: elapsed time until convergence, number of multiplications, performance-degradation factors, and maximum size of the physical memory occupied through the iterations.
elements.sent-2411-240: The accuracy of the clustering results was not our performance measure because the algorithms were just accelerations of the spherical $K$-means.
elements.sent-2411-241: We evaluated our proposed algorithm ES-ICP by comparing it with the baseline algorithm MIVI and three algorithms using state-of-the-art techniques in our setting.
elements.sent-2411-242: The first algorithm only uses the ICP filter, which is referred to as ICP.
elements.sent-2411-243: The second algorithm combined a method inspired by Fagin$^+$'s TA \cite{fagin01 and Li$^+$'s cosine-threshold algorithm \cite{li with ICP (TA-ICP).}}
elements.sent-2411-244: The third algorithm incorporated the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality as in Bottesch$^+$ \cite{bottesch or Knittel$^+$ \cite{knittel to ICP (CS-ICP).}}
elements.sent-2411-245: We briefly describe TA-ICP and CS-ICP (detailed in Appendix~\ref{app:compalgo ).}
elements.sent-2411-246: Both algorithms used the identical framework as ES-ICP for a fair comparison, based on a three-region partition in their mean-inverted indexes and two phases for gathering candidates and their verification.
elements.sent-2411-247: Incorporating parameter $t^{}$ leads to save the required memory size of $\breve{\mathcal M ^{p[r]}$ at the verification phase in Section~\ref{ssec:icp .}}
elements.sent-2411-248: The $t^{}$ was preset at $0.9\,D$, which was close to the value obtained by our estimation algorithm in Section~\ref{sec:strparam .}
elements.sent-2411-249: TA-ICP and CS-ICP differed from ES-ICP mostly in the following four points:.
elements.sent-2411-250: TA-ICP employs an individual structural parameter ({\em threshold ) on the mean-feature values for each object while our algorithm uses the common structural parameter $v^{}$, which is shared among all the objects.}
elements.sent-2411-251: The individual threshold $v_{(ta)i ^{}$ for the $i$th object is defined by \begin{equation}v_{(ta)i}^{} = \rho_{(max)}/\| {\bm x}_i\|_1\, , \label{eq:ta_th}\end{equation} where $\rho_{(max)}$ denotes the similarity of the $i$th object to the centroid to which the object is assigned at the last iteration and $\|{\bm x _i\|_1$ the $L_1$-norm of the object, i.e., $\|{\bm x _i\|_1\!=\!\sum_{p=1 ^{(nt)_i u_{t_{(i,p) }$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-252: Upper bound $\rho_{(j;i) ^{[ub]}$ on the similarity to the $j$th centroid, which is used in the main filter, is expressed by \begin{eqnarray} \rho_{(j;i)}^{[ub]} = (\rho 1)_{(j;i)} +(\rho 2')_{(j;i)} +v_{(ta)i}^{}\cdot y_{(i,j)}\footnotemark \label{eq:ta-ub} \hskip1.2em y_{(i,j)}=\sum_{s\geq t^{}}\!u_{(s,j)}\,, \hskip1.2em s=t_{(i,p)}\,,\end{eqnarray} \footnotetext{To this multiplication, the scaling like that in ES-ICP\,\footref{footnote:scaling for reducing multiplications cannot be applied due to using the individual threshold.}}}
elements.sent-2411-253: The upper bound $\rho_{(j;i) ^{[ub]}$ in Eq.~(\ref{eq:ta-ub ) works as the UBP filter, compared with $\rho_{(max)}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-254: TA-ICP uses a special {\em partial mean-inverted index, each array of which is sorted in descending order of mean-feature values, and calculates a part of $(\rho 2')_{(j;i)}$ while going down in the array from the top to the position of threshold $v_{(ta)i ^{}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-255: To combine the main filter TA with ICP, an {\em additional sorted mean-inverted index only for moving centroids was incorporated because TA-ICP needs a sorted mean-inverted index.}
elements.sent-2411-256: For calculating $(\rho 2')_{(j;i)}$, the additional sorted mean-inverted index is used if the $i$th object is more similar in Eq.~(\ref{eq:objstate ), otherwise, the sorted mean-inverted index is done.}
elements.sent-2411-257: TA-ICP prepares another partial mean-inverted index for calculating exact similarities of centroids passing through the filters, which corresponds to $\breve{M ^{p[r]}$ in ES-ICP (Table~\ref{table:nota_prop ) but differs in that it has all the mean-feature values due to using the individual threshold $v_{(ta)i ^{}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-258: When calculating the exact similarity, it has to skip with conditional branches the mean-feature values larger than or equal to $v_{(ta)i ^{}$ that are already being utilized for $(\rho 2')_{(j;i)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-259: TA-ICP suffers from the disadvantages of more BMs and LLCMs in Table~\ref{table:comp .}
elements.sent-2411-260: These are attributed to properties of the TA algorithm itself, that is, finding out a termination point of the gathering phase in its sorted inverted index like Fagin$^+$ and Li$^+$.
elements.sent-2411-261: The BMs were caused by the conditional branches that irregularly return their judgments of whether or not a current point is the termination one.
elements.sent-2411-262: The LLCMs were induced by simultaneously using the three distinct arrays, which were for the partial similarity $(\rho 1)_{(j:i)}\!+(\rho 2')_{(j;i)}$, the partial mean-inverted index, and the additional one.
elements.sent-2411-263: CS-ICP employs upper bound $\rho_{(j;i) ^{[ub]}$ on the similarity to the $j$th centroid based on the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality in Regions 2 and 3, which is expressed by \begin{eqnarray} \rho_{(j;i)}^{[ub]} = (\rho 1)_{(j;i)} + \|{\bm x}_i^{p}\|_2 \times \sqrt{\|{\bm \mu}_{(j;i)}^{p}\|_2^2}\label{eq:cs-ub} \hskip1.2em \|{\bm x}_i^{p}\|_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{t_{(i,p)}\geq t^{}} u_{t_{(i,p)}}^2} \hskip1.2em \|{\bm \mu}_{(j;i)}^{p}\|_2^2 = \sum_{\substack{t_{(i,p)}\geq t^{} s\gets t_{(i,p)}}} v_{c_{(s,q)}}^2 , \hskip1.2em j = c_{(s,q)}\, ,\end{eqnarray} where the second term on the right-hand side in Eq.~(\ref{eq:cs-ub ) is the upper bound on the partial similarity in the subspace spanned by the bases of the $i$th object's inherent dimensions of $t_{(i,p)}\!\geq \!t^{}$.}}
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}\rho_{(j;i)}^{[ub]} = (\rho 1)_{(j;i)} + \|{\bm x}_i^{p}\|_2 \times \sqrt{\|{\bm \mu}_{(j;i)}^{p}\|_2^2}\label{eq:cs-ub} \hskip1.2em \|{\bm x}_i^{p}\|_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{t_{(i,p)}\geq t^{}} u_{t_{(i,p)}}^2} \hskip1.2em \|{\bm \mu}_{(j;i)}^{p}\|_2^2 = \sum_{\substack{t_{(i,p)}\geq t^{} s\gets t_{(i,p)}}} v_{c_{(s,q)}}^2 , \hskip1.2em j = c_{(s,q)}\, ,\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-279: ES-ICP significantly suppressed the elapsed time compared to the others from the early to the last stage in the iterations.
elements.sent-2411-280: As a result, ES-ICP worked over 15 times faster than MIVI and at least 3.5 times faster than the others.
elements.sent-2411-281: From the values in the three evaluation items of Inst, BM, and LLCM, we know that ES-ICP was designed so as to operate in the AFM.
elements.sent-2411-282: By contrast, TA-ICP and CS-ICP needed more elapsed time than ICP although they had better filters.
elements.sent-2411-283: Their inferior speed-performances are attributed to the difficulty of designing the algorithms in the AFM.
elements.sent-2411-284: Compared with ICP, CS-ICP and TA-ICP caused many last-level-cache load misses (LLCM) because these algorithms needed additional data loaded to the limited-capacity caches.
elements.sent-2411-285: CS-ICP stored the squared mean-feature values in the partial squared-mean-inverted index whose term IDs ($s$) were in the range of $t^{}\!\leq\! s\!\leq\! D$.
elements.sent-2411-286: TA-ICP stored the mean-feature values of the moving centroids in the additional mean-inverted index for all the term IDs.
elements.sent-2411-287: TA-ICP also caused many branch mispredictions (BM) due to the two additional conditional branches for terminating the exact partial similarity calculation in Region 2 and avoiding the double similarity calculations for the centroids passing through its main filter.
elements.sent-2411-288: Thus, the proposed algorithm ES-ICP achieved higher performance by reducing the performance-degradation factors of Mult, Inst, BM, and LLCM.
elements.sent-2411-289: Regarding the memory usage in Table~\ref{table:comp , the three algorithms of ES-ICP, CS-ICP, and TA-ICP needed around double the maximum memory size (Max MEM) of MIVI and ICP since they used partial mean-inverted index $\breve{\mathcal M ^{p[r]}$ for exact similarity calculations for centroids passing through their filters at the verification phase, which is designed so as to work in the AFM.}}
elements.sent-2411-290: To suppress the maximum memory size, we have to develop new techniques.
elements.sent-2411-291: We discuss why the ES-ICP algorithm works efficiently from the two viewpoints of the AFM in Section~\ref{sec:arch and the effective use of the UCs of large-scale and high-dimensional sparse document data sets in Section~\ref{sec:dchar .}}
elements.sent-2411-292: First, we classify the algorithms and highlight their characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-293: Next, we describe the efficient operation of our ES filter, relating with both the feature-value-concentrarion phenomenon and the estimated structural parameter (threshold) $v^{}$.
elements.sent-2411-294: Last, we show that ES-ICP efficiently works in the AFM for not only the PubMed but also the NYT data set based on their UCs.
elements.sent-2411-295: Regarding these two viewpoints, Table~\ref{table:algoclass shows a coarse classification of the algorithms using the mean-inverted index in Sections~\ref{ssec:compalgo .}}
elements.sent-2411-296: The rows and columns respectively represent the two classes of whether the algorithms effectively used the UCs or not and the three classes of how the algorithms were designed so as to operate in the AFM.
elements.sent-2411-297: Note, however, that all the algorithms are designed to some level in the AFM since they adopt their structured mean-inverted index instead of the data-object-inverted index in Section~\ref{sec:arch .}
elements.figure-2411-013: \caption{ Algorithm classification} \label{table:algoclass} \begin{tabular}{|c||c|c|c|} Effective use of \multicolumn{3}{c|}{Architecture friendly} UCs High Moderate Low Good ES-ICP CS-ICP TA-ICP Poor - ICP MIVI \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-298: MIVI is classified into the class where UCs are not used and with a low-level design with respect to the AFM.
elements.sent-2411-299: This is because MIVI without an ICP filter cannot reduce the instructions even at the last stage in the iterations.
elements.sent-2411-300: Furthermore, continuing almost the constant number of the instructions causes that of cache misses to last.
elements.sent-2411-301: ICP does not effectively use the UCs even though it is designed in a moderate-level AFM.
elements.sent-2411-302: It suppresses the number of instructions from the middle and last stages in the iteration owing to its filter and the number of the branch mispredictions by its structured mean-inverted-index without conditional branches to judge whether or not each centroid is invariant.
elements.sent-2411-303: ES-ICP, CS-ICP, and TA-ICP effectively utilize the UCs by partitioning the mean-inverted index into three regions in Fig.~\ref{fig:regions .}
elements.sent-2411-304: However, they are classified into the different classes on the AFM for their different main UBP filters.
elements.sent-2411-305: ES-ICP is designed in a high-level AFM.
elements.sent-2411-306: It needs neither additional data nor conditional branches for its main filter, which leverages the common $v^{}$ shared with all the objects in Section~\ref{sec:prop .}
elements.sent-2411-307: By contrast, since CS-ICP and TA-ICP respectively use their additional data for the squared mean-feature-value arrays and the sorted invariant and moving mean-feature-value arrays, they suffer many cache misses.
elements.sent-2411-308: Moreover, TA-ICP employs individual threshold $v_{(ta)i ^{}$ for every object in Eq.~(\ref{eq:ta_th ).}}
elements.sent-2411-309: It needs the additional conditional branch for judging the magnitude relationship between $v_{(ta)i ^{}$ and a mean-feature value, resulting in increasing the branch mispredictions.}
elements.sent-2411-310: For these reasons, CS-ICP and TA-ICP are classified into the moderate- and low-level classes on the AFM.

elements.sent-2411-341: This section reviews the algorithms and techniques that are closely related to our proposed algorithm in two distinct topics, Lloyd-type $K$-means clustering algorithms and search algorithms with inverted indexes, and describes their drawbacks and availability in our settings.
elements.sent-2411-342: Many Lloyd-type acceleration algorithms have been studied for dense object-feature vectors in a metric space \cite{pelleg,kaukoranta,elkan,hamerly_book,bottesch,hattori,xia .}
elements.sent-2411-343: Their key strategies are using lower bounds on distances between an object and centroids for skipping unnecessary distance calculations.
elements.sent-2411-344: Elkan's \cite{elkan and Hamerly's algorithms \cite{hamerly and their variants \cite{drake,ding,rysavy,newling are typical examples based on the former key strategy.}}}
elements.sent-2411-345: They leverage the distances calculated at the previous iteration and the moving distances between identical centroids at two consecutive iterations (Appendix~\ref{app:triangle ).}
elements.sent-2411-346: However, using the moving distance limits the acceleration in the last stage at the iterations where most centroids slightly move or become invariant.
elements.sent-2411-347: Among them, Drake$^+$ \cite{drake and Ding$^+$ \cite{ding make the lower bounds tighter using distinct structural parameters proportional to $K$.}}
elements.sent-2411-348: Given a large $K$ value like in our setting, they need a large amount of memory and are not practical.
elements.sent-2411-349: Bottesch$^+$ algorithm \cite{bottesch uses the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality to obtain the lower bound on a Euclidean distance between data-object- and mean-feature vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-350: To tighten the bound, the algorithm partitions a given feature space into disjoint subspaces whose number is an empirical parameter.
elements.sent-2411-351: The algorithm is inefficient if it is na\"{i vely applied to document data with small values of $(\hat{D /D)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-352: This is because the set-intersection size of the terms in an object and a centroid is very small, and then the bound by this algorithm is loosened.
elements.sent-2411-353: To avoid such inefficiency, a subspace might be used where an object-feature vector is contained instead of pre-defined subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-354: This approach, however, is also inefficient because of the results of CS-ICP (Section~\ref{ssec:perfcomp ).}
elements.sent-2411-355: Newling$^+$ \cite{newling and Xia$^+$ algorithms \cite{xia structure a centroid set.}}
elements.sent-2411-356: Exponion in Newling$^+$ eliminates centroids outside a ball defined for each object from distance-calculation candidates.
elements.sent-2411-357: The ball's center is a centroid of a cluster whose radius is derived based on the triangle inequality.
elements.sent-2411-358: Xia$^+$ algorithm regards a previous cluster as a ball and narrows down the distance-calculation candidates using a geometric relationship of the balls.
elements.sent-2411-359: A ball's center is a centroid, and its radius is the distance to the farthest object in the cluster.
elements.sent-2411-360: These algorithms are unsuitable for high-dimensional data sets like document data because they were originally developed for low-dimensional ones.
elements.sent-2411-361: Furthermore, they need to store centroid-centroid distances with $\mathcal{O (K^2)$ memory consumption, which is prohibited in our setting where large $K$ values are used.}
elements.sent-2411-362: Kaukoranta$^+$ algorithm \cite{kaukoranta using its ICP filter deals with a special case where the moving distance is zero.}
elements.sent-2411-363: Due to its simplicity, it is used as an auxiliary filter in several algorithms \cite{hattori,bottesch,knittel,xia .}
elements.sent-2411-364: We also use it because it is easily implemented for an inverted index in the AFM.
elements.sent-2411-365: A threshold algorithm (TA) \cite{fagin01 is a well-known classic algorithm with guaranteed optimality.}
elements.sent-2411-366: TA's inverted-index consists of arrays called postings lists each entry of which is sorted in descending order of its feature value.
elements.sent-2411-367: TA collects document ID's in the postings until each of the moving cursors on the postings lists reaches a pre-defined position for a stopping condition of its gathering phase.
elements.sent-2411-368: The strategy needs to execute many conditional branches.
elements.sent-2411-369: If TA is applied to $K$-means clustering, it does not always reduce the elapsed time.
elements.sent-2411-370: In fact, the TA-ICP algorithm, inspired by TA in Section~\ref{ssec:perfcomp , required more elapsed time than the ICP algorithm without any UBP filter although TA-ICP reduced the multiplications.}
elements.sent-2411-371: A TA-based algorithm \cite{li was recently reported that utilizes a complete and tight stopping condition and a traversal strategy with a near-optimal access cost over its inverted index.}
elements.sent-2411-372: Despite the near-optimal access cost, the algorithm uses many conditional branches like TA and also needs additional computational costs to achieve near-optimality.
elements.sent-2411-373: It does not take into account any negative impacts of the performance-degradation factors.
elements.sent-2411-374: This casts doubts on the algorithm's actual speed-performance.
elements.sent-2411-375: Fast algorithms in the document-at-a-time (DAAT) query evaluation such as WAND \cite{broder03}\footnote{ The original algorithm is heuristic and out of our scope.}
elements.sent-2411-376: For the same reason as the TA-based algorithm, they suffer from such tail queries as long queries in our settings although they may achieve an expected performance for short queries.
elements.sent-2411-377: This is attributed to their dynamic skipping techniques, which are far from the operation in the AFM in Section~\ref{sec:arch .}
elements.sent-2411-378: Irregularly skipping postings by their conditional branches caused many branch mispredictions and cache misses \cite{crane .}
elements.sent-2411-379: We proposed the accelerated spherical $K$-means clustering algorithm ES-ICP with a large $K$ value, which is suitable for large-scale and high-dimensional sparse document data sets such as the 8.2M-sized PubMed and 1M-sized NYT data sets.
elements.sent-2411-380: ES-ICP efficiently works with the structured mean-inverted index and the two filters of the novel ES and auxiliary ICP that are designed using the universal characteristics (UCs) of the document data sets so as to operate in the architecture-friendly manner (AFM).
elements.sent-2411-381: We presented the estimation algorithm for the two structural parameters that partition the structured mean-inverted index into three regions, resulting in a tight upper bound on the similarity between the object- and mean-feature vectors.
elements.sent-2411-382: By using the tight upper bound, the ES filter achieved high pruning rates in all the iterations.
elements.sent-2411-383: We confirmed that ES-ICP performed over 15 times and at least three times faster than the baseline MIVI and the compared algorithms using the state-of-the-art techniques for the 8.2M-sized PubMed, given $K\!=\!80\,000$, respectively.

elements.sent-2411-456: We analyze the contributions of components in our proposed algorithm ES-ICP to its performance.
elements.sent-2411-457: We focus on the main filter ES since the performance of only the auxiliary filter ICP is evaluated in Section~\ref{ssec:perfcomp .}
elements.sent-2411-458: The ES filter exploits the tight upper bounds on the similarities obtained by using the three regions divided by the two parameters of $t^{}$ and $v^{}$.
elements.sent-2411-459: Depending on whether or not the two parameters are used, we prepared three algorithms without ICP in addition to the baseline MIVI.
elements.sent-2411-460: The first algorithm was ES that employed both the two parameters.
elements.sent-2411-461: The second was ThV that utilized only the parameter of $v^{}$ that is estimated by $t^{}\!=\!0$ \footnote{ ThV's $v^{}$ was 0.032 while ES's 0.038 for 8.2M-sized PubMed with $K\!=\!80\,000$.. .}
elements.sent-2411-462: ThV calculated the upper-bound similarities based on the $v^{}$ in the range of all the term IDs.
elements.sent-2411-463: The upper bounds were looser than those of ES since the exact partial similarities in Region 1 of ES were replaced with the corresponding upper bounds.
elements.sent-2411-464: For calculating the exact similarities of unpruned centroids, ThV needed mean-inverted index $\breve{M ^{p[r]}$ of the memory size of $\{ D\times K\times sizeof(double) \$ bytes.}}
elements.sent-2411-465: Note that ThV's memory size differs from that of ES-ICP, which is $\{ (D\!-\!t^{}\!+\!1)\times K\times sizeof(double) \$ bytes.}
elements.sent-2411-466: The last was ThT that utilized the parameter of $t^{}$ that is estimated by $v^{}\!=\!1.0$ \footnote{ ThT's $t^{}$ was 140\,904 while ES's 128\,090 for 8.2M-sized PubMed with $K\!=\!80\,000$.. .}
elements.sent-2411-467: The upper bounds $\rho_{(j;i) ^{[ub]'}$ corresponding to those in Eq.~(\ref{eq:ub ) are expressed by $\rho_{(j;i) ^{[ub]'}\!=\!(\rho 1')_{(j;i) +\|{\bm x _i^{p'}\|_1$, where $\|{\bm x _i^{p' \|$ and $(\rho 1')_{(j;i)}$ respectively denote the partial $L_1$ norm of the $i$th object and the exact partial similarity of the object to the $j$th centroid based on ThT's $t^{}$.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-494: This section details TA-ICP and CS-ICP in Section~\ref{ssec:perfcomp .}
elements.sent-2411-495: Both the algorithms employ the mean-inverted index that is partitioned by the structural parameter $t^{}$ (threshold on term IDs) into two regions like the proposed algorithm ES-ICP.
elements.sent-2411-496: The differences from ES-ICP are in their main upper-bound-based pruning (UBP) filters and data structures related to the filters.
elements.sent-2411-497: TA-ICP is characterized by using (1) the UBP filter every an individual object and (2) two mean-inverted indexes for moving centroids and all the centroids.
elements.sent-2411-498: Each array of the mean-inverted index is sorted in descending order of the mean-feature values.
elements.sent-2411-499: Remind that ES-ICP's array is not sorted but classified in Section~\ref{ssec:frame .}
elements.sent-2411-500: The UBP filter of TA-ICP is designed, inspired by the threshold algorithm (TA) in Fagin$^+$ and Li$^+$ algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-501: This filter utilizes a threshold ($v_{(ta)i ^{}$) on mean-feature values, which is defined every the $i$th object by Eq.~(\ref{eq:ta_th ) as follows. \begin{equation}v_{(ta)i}^{} = \rho_{(max)}/\|{\bm x}_i \|_1 \, , \nonumber\end{equation} where $\|{\bm x _i\|_1$ and $\rho_{(max)}$ denote the $L_1$-norm of the $i$th object-feature vector and the similarity of $i$th object to the centroid to which the object belongs at the last iteration.}}}
elements.sent-2411-502: Due to the threshold for the individual object, it is difficult to structure the mean-inverted index when incorporating the auxiliary ICP filter unlike ES-ICP.
elements.sent-2411-503: TA-ICP employs the two mean-inverted indexes for moving and all the centroids.
